coredata.dc:title: Nickel doping: Improving the optical properties of carbon dots and increasing the sensitivity for detecting pH and water
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Li
Hao, Yumin
Liu, Yang
Dong, Yueping
Chen, Zexian
Dong, Wenjuan
Zhao, Zhonghua
Hu, Qin
Dong, Chuan
Gong, Xiaojuan
coredata.dc:description: pH and water play a crucial role in chemical, environmental and physiological monitoring processes, so it is of great importance to establish a simple and sensitive multi-mode detection method. In this study, we fabricated nickel-doped carbon dots (Ni-CDs) by a hydrothermal method using nickel chloride (NiCl2•6 H2O) and m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA) as precursors, which exhibits excellent photostability, outstanding resistance to photobleaching and ultra-low cytotoxicity. Ni doping increased the fluorescence quantum yield (from 21.4% to 30.3%) and H content (from 44.4% to 57.1%) of the Ni-CDs, making them highly sensitive to pH and water, which facilitated the detection of pH and water based on deprotonation and aggregation mechanisms, respectively. Ni-CDs can detect pH in the ranges of 6.0–9.0 and 6.0–11.0 via fluorometric and smartphone methods, respectively. The limits of detection (LODs) of the Ni-CDs in DMSO, DMF and THF for the detection of water were 0.0112%, 0.0144% and 0.0060%, respectively. Moreover, the Ni-CDs have been effectively utilized for pH detection in real samples as well as for pH in cells and zebrafish with satisfactory results. More importantly, the Ni-CDs/hydrogel remains perfectly the sensitive to pH and water, broadening the applications of the CDs in sensing, bioimaging and information encryption.

head.ce:title: Nickel doping: Improving the optical properties of carbon dots and increasing the sensitivity for detecting pH and water

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: pH and water play a crucial role in chemical, environmental and physiological monitoring processes, so it is of great importance to establish a simple and sensitive multi-mode detection method. In this study, we fabricated nickel-doped carbon dots (Ni-CDs) by a hydrothermal method using nickel chloride (NiCl2•6 H2O) and m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA) as precursors, which exhibits excellent photostability, outstanding resistance to photobleaching and ultra-low cytotoxicity. Ni doping increased the fluorescence quantum yield (from 21.4% to 30.3%) and H content (from 44.4% to 57.1%) of the Ni-CDs, making them highly sensitive to pH and water, which facilitated the detection of pH and water based on deprotonation and aggregation mechanisms, respectively. Ni-CDs can detect pH in the ranges of 6.0–9.0 and 6.0–11.0 via fluorometric and smartphone methods, respectively. The limits of detection (LODs) of the Ni-CDs in DMSO, DMF and THF for the detection of water were 0.0112%, 0.0144% and 0.0060%, respectively. Moreover, the Ni-CDs have been effectively utilized for pH detection in real samples as well as for pH in cells and zebrafish with satisfactory results. More importantly, the Ni-CDs/hydrogel remains perfectly the sensitive to pH and water, broadening the applications of the CDs in sensing, bioimaging and information encryption.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are promising for a wide application range in optical sensing [1–4], bioimaging [2,5], drug delivery [6,7], anti-counterfeiting [5], information encryption [5], etc. due to their excellent luminescence, high photostability, simple synthesis and facile surface modification. Heteroatom doping, especially nonmetallic heteroatoms including N [5,8], Cl [8], P [8], S [5,8], etc., is considered as an effective approach to regulate the surface features and luminescence properties of materials [5,9]. However, recent researches have revealed that CDs doped with metallic heteroatoms (like iron [10], lanthanum [11], copper [12], zinc [13], gadolinium [7,14], nickel [15–18], etc.) can exhibit attractive electrical and optical characteristics. It has been shown that Ni-doping can significantly increase the surface oxidation and fluorescence quantum yield of CDs [16].

Due to the high fluorescence quantum yield of Ni-doped CDs, applications based on the fluorescence of Ni-doped CDs have attracted much attention and received extensive research. Ni doping carbon dots prepared by Sajid et al. via one-pot microwave-assisted pyrolysis have ferromagnetic saturation behavior and may provide a promising magneto-fluorescent nanoprobe for bimodal bioimaging [15]. Ni-pPCDs synthesized from p-phenylenediamine and nickel precursors exhibit excellent fluorescence characteristics, enabling real-time, wash-free and high-resolution nuclear imaging, as well as high-contrast imaging of tumor-bearing mice and zebrafish [16]. In addition, CDs doping with nickel and nitrogen have been utilized as photothermal, photoacoustic and magnetic resonance imaging contrast agents because of their outstanding photothermal effect and instinctive paramagnetic feature [17]. As can be seen, the excellent fluorescence properties of Ni-doped CDs have attracted much attention in bioimaging, but they have not yet emerged in the sensing field. Therefore, we want to prepare novel Ni-doped CDs and try to explore their applications in sensing and further extend them to bioimaging and information encryption.

pH plays a crucial role in chemical analysis, environment and physiological processes [19–22]. Subtle changes in pH may affect the synthesis and properties of materials [23] or impact soil stability and water safety [24], and may even influence cell proliferation, apoptosis evasion, metabolic adaptation and cell migration [25], indicating that it is very critical to detect pH changes. The pH sensors constructed based on the CDs basically achieves pH detection by fluorescence change [20,21,26–30], which still has some limitations in practical applications. Visual pH sensors allow on-site inspection by color change without the need for large instrumentation [20], but the method is highly susceptible to subjective factors, such as differences in color recognition by the human eye, resulting in a dramatic decrease in the accuracy of results [3,22]. Smartphones are widely used in the field of sensing due to their superior imaging capabilities and powerful computing abilities [22,31], which can not only overcome the shortcomings of naked eye observation, but also do not require the assistance of large instrumentation. Therefore, it is of great theoretical significance and practical value to construct a sensor based on the CDs for dual-mode detection of pH by fluorescence and smartphone, and to deeply study the application of this sensor in the field of bioimaging and information encryption.

In organic reactions, water is often considered a contaminant or impurity that adversely affects the chemical reaction or the properties of the chemical product [32–34]. The determination of water content in organic solutions is essential for the monitoring organic synthesis processes, especially in the fields of pharmaceutical manufacturing, food processing, environmental monitoring, etc [34,35]. To overcome the shortcomings of the conventional Karl Fischer titration method for the water content detection in organic solutions, such as the complexity of the operation and the need for specialized apparatus (anhydrous titration tank) and trained personnel [32,34], there is an urgent need to develop a convenient, simple and reliable method for detecting water so as to facilitate its application in many fields such as organic synthesis. Although CDs-based fluorescence methods have been developed for water detection because of the simple operation, fast response and high sensitivity, fluorescence detection alone can still be limited in practical applications [32,33,35–38]. Consequently, we hope to establish a sensor for dual-mode detection of water based on the CDs with fluorescence and smartphone, which is conducive to the rapid on-site detection of water content in organic solvents.

Herein, it is proposed to synthesize nickel-doped carbon dots (Ni-CDs) with green fluorescence, low cytotoxicity and extraordinary photostability by a one-pot hydrothermal method using nickel chloride (NiCl2•6 H2O) and m-phenylenediamine (m-PDA) as raw materials. Based on the Ni-CDs, it is suggested to construct sensors for detecting targets by changes in fluorescence intensity, absorbance and color. It is worth noting that the fluorescence intensity of the obtained Ni-CDs can response to pH and water content, accompanied by significant color changes. Therefore, a simple and fast dual-mode sensor based on fluorescence and smartphone can be established to detect pH and water. The ultra-low cytotoxicity of the Ni-CDs allows for pH bioimaging in cells and zebrafish. Ultimately, the established Ni-CDs/hydrogel sensor responses to both pH and water and performs well in information encryption. This work perfectly demonstrates the incomparably rich application potential of the CDs and has potential to provide a completely new idea for the further development of Ni-doped CDs. Scheme 1.

The Ni-CDs were obtained by a simple hydrothermal method. Briefly, NiCl2•6H2O (0.06 g) and m-PDA (0.03 g) were added into absolute ethanol (10 mL) to form a homogeneous solution and transferred into a Teflon-lined autoclave (50 mL) and reacted for 6 h at 160 °C. After removing ethanol from the reaction solution by rotary evaporation method, it is redissolved in ultrapure water to acquire the brownish solution. Next, the brownish solution was centrifuged (8000 rpm, 10 min) to remove impurities. The clarified supernatant extract was then dialyzed against ultrapure water in a dialysis bag (500–1000 Da) for 24 h. Finally, the black solid powder of the obtained Ni-CDs after freezing was stored at 4 °C for further use. Using the same method, Ni-free doped CDs (m-CDs) were synthesized employing 0.03 g of m-PDA, and comparative experiments were carried out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH detection and the application in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: 10 μL of Ni-CDs (10 mg mL−1) were added to 2.0 mL of PBS with different pHs and their fluorescence emission spectra were acquired under the excitation wavelength of 385 nm. Measurements were conducted in triplicate. In order to ensure the stability of light source when capturing images, a fixed 365 nm UV lamp in the darkroom was chosen to simulate the UV light source, while a fixed angle and position were selected to take the images to ensure the image quality while minimizing background interference. Images of Ni-CDs solutions with different pHs were taken by smartphone camera under UV light and then analyzed using software installed on the smartphone (Color Identification and WPS Office).

To verify the feasibility of pH detection based on the Ni-CDs, we selected river water from Lvliang River of China, tap water from Taiyuan of China, pH standard solution, tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane hydrochloride (Tric-HCl) buffer and sodium acetate (NaAc) buffer as the actual samples to analyze the respective pH and compared with the results of pH meters. Firstly, the pHs of the real samples were detected by a pH meter. Then, the pH value of the real samples was measured again with the constructed pH sensor. All the tests were repeated three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of water content in organic solvents based on the Ni-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The Ni-CDs powder was dissolved into organic solvents (DMSO, DMF and THF) containing different amounts of water, and the final concentration of the Ni-CDs was controlled to 0.05 mg mL−1. The emission spectra of the above solutions were recorded under 385 nm excitation. The method of detecting water content in organic solvents based on the Ni-CDs using smartphones is similar to its method of detecting pH.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ni-CDs-based paper sensing for pH and water detection
ce:section.ce:para: The filter paper was soaked in the Ni-CDs solution (0.05 mg mL−1) for 2 min and then dried in an oven (37 °C, 5 min), after which it was cut into 1.0 cm2 squares. Aqueous solutions of different pHs and organic solvents with various water contents were added dropwise to the surface of the filter paper, and the images were captured and analyzed by a smartphone.

Human cervical carcinoma (HeLa) cells were cultured in DMEM culture medium at 37 °C and in air containing 5% CO2 for 24 h. DMEM was replaced with the Ni-CDs aqueous solution (0.05 mg mL−1) or Ni-CDs solution (0.05 mg mL−1) of different pHs (6.0, 7.0 and 9.0) and HeLa cells were incubated another 2 h. HeLa cells were washed three times with Dulbecco’s Phosphate Buffered Saline (DPBS, pH = 7.4) and kept in DPBS (500 μL) before imaging HeLa cells by a confocal laser scanning microscope (LSM710, Zeiss, Germany).

The zebrafish imaging experiments were approved by Committee of Scientific Research in Shanxi University and conducted in strict accordance with animal welfare guidelines. Four-day-old zebrafish were incubated with the Ni-CDs aqueous solution (1.0 mg mL−1) or Ni-CDs aqueous solution (1.0 mg mL−1) in different pHs for 2 h, then washed three times with DPBS, and finally zabrafish photographs were collected by laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM).

The 4% hydrogel was synthesized by microwave method according to the following steps: 0.4 g of agarose was dissolved in 20 mL of ultrapure water and microwaved until the mass of the final solution was 10 g. Then 0.5 mg Ni-CDs solid powder was added to the solution and rapidly stirred well. Finally, Ni-CDs/hydrogels were obtained by pouring the above solution into a dish and cooling it. Ni-CDs/hydrogels were placed in beakers containing 14% ammonium hydroxide solution and 14% hydrochloric acid solution, respectively, and heated in a water bath at 70 °C for 5 min. The response of Ni-CDs/hydrogels to different pHs was simulated by recording the changes of hydrogels at different time points. The organic solvents have a high boiling point and are not volatile. They need to be dropped directly on the Ni-CDs/hydrogel and allowed to penetrate into the hydrogel after a certain period of time. Afterwards, the response of the Ni-CDs/hydrogel to different water contents in organic solvents was simulated by recording the phenomenon of hydrogels at different times. All pictures were taken by a smartphone camera under UV irradiation at 365 nm.

Ni-CDs were fabricated using hydrothermal treatment of NiCl2•6 H2O and m-PDA. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image showed that the Ni-CDs uniformly distributed on the copper grid with a mean size of 2.1 nm ( Fig. 1a). The high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images of the Ni-CDs (inset in Fig. 1a and Fig. S1a) exhibited an obvious lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, assigning to the plane (100) of graphite carbon [1,2]. Characterization of the existence form of the Ni-CDs was conducted through small-angle X-ray scattering (SAXS) analysis (Fig. 1b). The results indicate that the low q contribution (q ≤ 0.4 nm−1) of the Ni-CDs is primarily due to the scattering of large-scale aggregates (red line), exhibiting behavior consistent with the Porod-law [39]. The full pattern fitting of the Ni-CDs (black line) reveals the presence of a significant number of nanoscale particles (green line), possibly attracted by large molecular aggregates [39]. In other words, the Ni-CDs are primarily composed of dispersed nanoscale particles with some inevitable aggregates, aligning well with the observations from TEM images. Elemental analysis, Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were utilized to investigate composition, elemental distribution and surface functional groups. As displayed in Table S1, the Ni-CDs were mainly composed of C (23.08%), N (8.13%), O (14.32%), Cl (39.9%), Ni (10.04%) and H (4.53%) with the empirical formula of C11N3H27O5Cl7Ni. Among them, the content of Ni (10.4%) in the Ni-CDs was determined by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) method. In order to confirm the successful synthesis of the Ni-CDs, the FTIR spectra of NiCl2•6 H2O, m-PDA, m-CDs and Ni-CDs were studied in detail and depicted in Fig. 1c. The O-H/N-H stretching vibration (3601–3014 cm−1) in the FTIR spectra of Ni-CDs and m-CDs were also found in the FTIR spectra of m-PDA and NiCl2•6 H2O, showing that the stretching vibration may originate from the reaction precursors. Compared with m-CDs, the C-O/CN stretching vibration peak of the Ni-CDs was shifted from 1623 cm−1 to 1635 cm−1, which may be due to the coordination of Ni with amino groups on the surface of the Ni-CDs [17]. Besides, a new stretching vibration of C-OH (1402 cm−1) different from that of m-CDs was found in the FTIR spectrum of the Ni-CDs, illustrating that the doping of Ni may have changed the surface structure of the Ni-CDs. The strong signals of O1s (530.0 eV), N1s (397.0 eV), C1s (282.0 eV) and Cl2p (195.0 eV) and weak signal of Ni2p (870.0 eV) were depicted in the XPS survey scan of the Ni-CDs (Fig. S1b). The C1s spectrum (Fig. 1d) was deconvoluted into four peaks at 283.4 eV (CC), 284.8 eV (C-C), 286.0 eV (C-N) and 288.2 eV (CO) [7,9,12,17]. The N1s spectrum (Fig. 1e) can be separated into three peaks at 398.8, 399.5 and 401.2 eV, which belong to CN, N-H and N-N = O, respectively [40,41]. The Ni2p spectrum shows four units (Fig. 1f) Ni2p3/2 (855.6 eV), Ni2p3/2 Sat. (861.3 and 864.4 eV), Ni2p1/2 (873.4 eV) and Ni2p1/2 Sat. (878.7 and 881.9 eV), revealing the presence of Ni in the Ni-CDs [17]. The O1s spectrum (Fig. S1c) illustrates three peaks at 530.8 eV, 531.6 eV and 533.1 eV, which are associated with CO, C-OH/C-O-C and C-O, respectively [42,43]. The Cl2p spectrum (Fig. S1d) can be divided into Cl-C (197.5 eV), bridging Cl (198.6 eV) and Cl-O (200.3 eV) [44–46]. Based on the above results, we speculate that the surface of the Ni-CDs may be covered with functional groups such as CC, CO, O-H, C-N, N-H, etc.

The optical characterizations of the obtained Ni-CDs were explored by fluorescence (FL) spectra, UV–vis absorption spectrum and quantum yield (QY). The optimal emission peak of the Ni-CDs under the excitation wavelength of 385 nm (blue line in Fig. 2a) is located at 492 nm (red line in Fig. 2a), and it emits bright green fluorescence under 365 nm UV illumination (the inset in Fig. 2a). Therefore, it is reasonable to set the excitation wavelength of the Ni-CDs at 385 nm throughout the experiment in order to achieve a high sensitivity. Excitation-dependent FL behavior can be observed when the excitation wavelength of the Ni-CDs is changed from 330 to 460 nm (Fig. 2b and S2a), which may be caused by the surface defect state of the Ni-CDs [5]. The UV–vis absorption spectrum of the Ni-CDs (black line in Fig. 2a) has four strong absorption peaks at 211, 256, 289 and 390 nm. The absorption peaks of the Ni-CDs at 211 nm and 256 nm are identified as π→π * transition of the aromatic sp2 bond [1,5], while the absorption peak at 289 nm is assigned to n→π * transition of the CO band [1]. In addition, the N-CDs exhibit a broad strong absorption peak at 390 nm, corresponding to the best FL excitation peak (Fig. 2a), which originates from the n→π * transition of the chemical groups on the surface of the Ni-CDs [5]. With fluorescein as the reference (Fig. S2b), the QY of the Ni-CDs was calculated to be 30.3% (Fig. S2c), which proved that the Ni-CDs have good luminescence efficiency.

The photostability of the Ni-CDs under Xe lamp irradiation, temperature, K+ and GSH was explored in detail. As shown in Fig. S3a, the Ni-CDs consistently exhibited excellent fluorescence stability during 120 min of Xe lamp irradiation. The relative FL intensity of the Ni-CDs gradually decreased with the increase of temperature from 25 °C to 70 °C, but remained at 89% when the temperature reached 70 °C, illustrating that the Ni-CDs have good temperature resistance (Fig. S3b). As shown in Fig. S3c and d, the influences of high ionic strength (K+, 2.0 mM) and GSH (10.0 mM) on the FL intensity of the Ni-CDs were negligible (≤ 10%), demonstrating that the Ni-CDs are able to tolerate high ionic strength and extreme biological environments and have potential applications in biological systems.

The first important finding was a gradual decrease in the FL intensity of the Ni-CDs as the pH changed from 3.0 to 12.0 ( Fig. 3a). The Boltzmann equation was used to fit the sigmoidal curve to obtain the equation y = 20.5477 + 694.5646/(1 + 10((pH – 7.6124)/0.5503)) (R2 = 0.9981) with a pKa of 7.61. A good linear relationship in the range of 6.0 ∼ 9.0 (Fig. 3b) with a linear equation of y = 2177.177 – 238.463 pH (R2 = 0.993). Moreover, the Ni-CDs with different pHs exhibited obvious color changes under UV lamp irradiation (the inset in Fig. 3c), therefore, a smartphone sensing probe for visual detection of pH was constructed based on the Ni-CDs using the photography and analysis functions of smartphones. Firstly, the fluorescence images of the Ni-CDs in different pHs were taken by a smartphone camera. Then, the images were recognized by smartphone software (Color Identification) to generate R (red), G (green) and B (blue) values. Finally, the R, G and B values were entered into the WPS software of the smartphone for analysis to obtain the linear relationship between RGB and pH. To realize the high sensitivity and reliability of the assay, G/B was chosen to establish a linear relationship with pH. As presented in Fig. 3c, the equation G/B = 1.456 + 0.421/(1 + 10((pH – 5.486)/1.720)) (R2 = 0.990) was obtained by fitting the Boltzmann equation on an sigmoidal curve with a pKa of 5.49. In the range of pH 6.0–11.0 (Fig. 3d), pH and G/B exhibits a good linear relationship with a linear equation of G/B = 1.347 – 0.028 pH (R2 = 0.989). To make the detection of pH easier and faster in practical applications, we constructed a reagent strip sensor based on the Ni-CDs for pH detection. After immersing the prepared Ni-CDs reagent strips in PBS with different pHs for 30 s, the fluorescence images were taken and analyzed with a smartphone. The relationship between G/B and pH was obtained and displayed in Fig. 3e with the sigmoidal-curve fitting equation and pKa of G/B = 0.0545 + 0.7292/(1 + 10((pH – 8.851)/1.3514)) (R2 = 0.979) and 8.85, respectively. As seen in Fig. 3f, G/B showed a good linear relationship with pH in the range of 6.0–11.0 with a linear equation of G/B = 1.373 – 0.108 pH (R2 = 0.975). The Ni-CDs-based pH sensor was compared with the previous CDs-based pH sensors, and the results are listed in Table S2. Compared with other CDs, the produced Ni-CDs have relatively high fluorescence quantum yield and can be used for a wider range of pH detection by various modes, which helps to achieve convenient, fast and accurate on-site detection.

The reversibility of the Ni-CDs to detect pH was investigated by adjusting the concentration of HCl or NaOH to switch the pH of the Ni-CDs solution between 6.0 and 9.0. No significant change of the FL intensity of the Ni-CDs can be found after five cycles, indicating that the Ni-CDs exhibited satisfactory reversibility to pH sensing (Fig. 3g). The anti-interference capability of the Ni-CDs is discussed to expand their application in cellular imaging. The FL intensity of the Ni-CDs solution in different pHs (6.0, 7.4 and 9.0) did not change significantly by Xe lamp irradiation for 90 min (Fig. S4) or in the presence of interfering substances (amino acids, metal ions and anions) (Fig. 3h), displaying that the Ni-CDs have outstanding pH stability and specificity. In order to better demonstrate that the constructed sensor based on the Ni-CDs can be used for pH detection in complex systems, we discuss the application of the Ni-CDs in actual samples, including river water, tap water, pH standard solution, Tric-HCl buffer and NaAC buffer samples. As presented in Table 1, the measurement results of the Ni-CDs-based sensor and pH meter are consistent, indicating that the constructed pH sensor based on the Ni-CDs provides a simpler, higher sensitive, and better stability method for pH detection in real samples.

The second meaningful finding is that the Ni-CDs exhibit completely different fluorescence properties in organic solutions with different polarities. Firstly, the fluorescence changes of the Ni-CDs in common organic solvents, including anhydrous ethanol, DMF, acetone, acetonitrile, DMSO, THF, ethyl acetate, dichloromethane (CH2Cl2) and trichloromethane (CHCl3), were discussed. Ni-CDs are insoluble in ethyl acetate, CH2Cl2 and CHCl3, which may be due to the weak polarity of the above three organic solvents. Although there is no obvious pattern between the polarity of organic solvents and the fluorescence of the Ni-CDs, it is interesting to note that the Ni-CDs have two emission peaks in the organic solvents DMF, DMSO and THF ( Fig. 4a), which may be caused by solvent effects. Based on this, a sensor for detecting the water content in DMF, DMSO and THF was constructed. As the water content in DMSO increased from 0% to 100%, the FL emission peak at 492 nm increased significantly, while the FL emission peak at 436 nm gradually decreased (Fig. 4b). The fluorescence intensity ratio (F492/F436) had a good linearity with water content in two linear ranges of 20–32% (Fig. 4c) and 40–56% (Fig. 4d) with the linear equation, R2 and the limit of detection (LOD) of F492/F436 = 0.1556 C 0.4298, 0.9976 and 0.0275% and F492/F436 = 0.3823 C 8.4246, 0.9910 and 0.0112%, respectively. The fluorescence changes of the Ni-CDs in DMF (Fig. S5a) and THF (Fig. S5d) with different water contents were similar to those in DMSO with different water contents. The linear relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F492/F429) of the Ni-CDs and the ratio of water when detecting water in DMF was F492/F429 = 0.0063 C + 0.4455 (R2 = 0.9905) and LOD of 0.6786% in the range of 2–15% (Fig. S5b), while in the range of 30–40%, it was F492/F429 = 0.2970 C 7.7465 (R2 = 0.9951) and LOD of 0.0144% (Fig. S5c). Similarly, the linear equations between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F492/F433) of the Ni-CDs and the water content in THF were F492/F433 = 0.1733 C 1.7855 (R2 = 0.9826) and F492/F433 = 0.7143 C 22.9563 (R2 = 0.9982), corresponding to the linear ranges and LODs were 15–40% (Fig. S5e) and 0.0247% and 40–70% (Fig. S5f) and 0.0060%, respectively. The relationship between G/B and water content in organic solvents was obtained by smartphone capture and analysis of the images (Fig. 4e, S5g and i) based on the significant change in fluorescence color of the Ni-CDs in organic solvents (DMSO, DMF and THF) with different water contents (inset of Fig. 4e, S5g and i). The fitted linear equations, linear ranges and LODs of G/B with water content in DMSO (Fig. 4f), DMF (Fig. S5h) and THF (Fig. S5j) were G/B = 0.024 C + 0.147 (R2 = 0.973), 0–50% and 6.5%, G/B = 0.039 C 0.644 (R2 = 0.992), 20–60% and 4.0% and G/B = 0.024 C 0.016 (R2 = 0.989), 15–60% and 6.5%, respectively. Likewise, Ni-CDs reagent strips have been used to detect water content in organic solutions (DMSO, DMF and THF) for simple and on-site detection. As the water content increased, the reagent strips changed from blue fluorescence to light green fluorescence (insets of Fig. 4g, S5k and m), which was extremely easy to identify and analyze by smartphone. A good linear relationship was established between G/B and water content in DMSO (Fig. 4h), DMF (Fig. S5l) and THF (Fig. S5n) with the linear equation, linear range and LOD of G/B = 0.012 C 0.279 (R2 = 0.928), 30–90% and 13%, G/B = 0.0124 C 0.262 (R2 = 0.999), 30–90% and 12.6% and G/B = 0.008 C + 0.753 (R2 = 0.928), 0–40% and 19.5%, respectively. Ni-CDs-based and reported CDs-based sensors for detecting water are compared in Table S3. The strategy of dual-mode sensing for water detection proposed in this work has extraordinary practicability and low LOD, which extends the application of the CDs in water detection and has great application potential in practice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanism of pH and water sensing
ce:section.ce:para: The pH and water sensing mechanism of the Ni-CDs was explored by the UV–vis absorption spectra, zeta potentials, TEM images and fluorescence lifetimes of the Ni-CDs in different pHs or organic solvents. The color of the Ni-CDs solution changed from yellowish to colorless with the pH value changed from 2.0 to 12.0 (inset in Fig. 5a), suggesting that the properties of the Ni-CDs may have changed. As depicted in Fig. 5a, there is an obvious difference in the UV–vis absorption spectra of the Ni-CDs solutions at various pHs. The absorption peak at 256 nm blue-shifted to 238 nm as the pH changed from 2.0 to 12.0 (Fig. 5b). In addition, the peak at 390 nm split into two peaks at 337 nm and 446 nm when the pH was changed from 2.0 to 10.0, while the peak at 390 nm was almost completely blue-shifted to 337 nm when the pH was 11.0 and 12.0 (Fig. 5c). Combined with the changes of solution color and absorption spectra of the Ni-CDs at different pHs, we speculate that the CC band and surface chemical groups of the Ni-CDs have changed significantly. As the pH increased from 2.0 to 10.0, the zeta potentials of the Ni-CDs decreased sharply from 11.8 eV to 18.2 eV (Fig. S6a), confirming the deprotonation process of the Ni-CDs [12]. The fluorescence lifetimes of the Ni-CDs decayed significantly with increasing pH (Fig. S6b), i.e., from 9.11 ns to 6.26 ns (Table S4), indicating that deprotonation of the Ni-CDs has an important effect on the fluorescence lifetime. In addition, we found that the size of the Ni-CDs at pH = 9.0 was 6.7 ± 0.5 nm (Fig. S6c), while the average size of the Ni-CDs in aqueous solution was 2.1 ± 0.3 nm (Fig. 1a). With increasing pH, the aggregation behavior of the Ni-CDs displayed non-covalent intermolecular interactions such as hydrogen bonding between carboxyl groups [47]. Besides, the aggregation behavior of the Ni-CDs results in their weaker fluorescence at higher pH [9,21,27]. Taken together, the sensing of pH by the Ni-CDs is mainly attributed to the deprotonation process and aggregation effect of the Ni-CDs in different pH solutions ( Scheme 2).

Surprisingly, the UV–vis absorption spectra changes of the Ni-CDs in different organic solvents were similar to those of the Ni-CDs at different pHs (Figs. S7a, b and 5d). The UV–vis absorption spectra of the Ni-CDs in DMF and DMSO have strong absorbance in the range of 240–270 nm, resulting in the variation of their absorption peaks at 256 nm being difficult to be accurately determined (Fig. S7b). However, the absorption peak at 256 nm was blue-shifted to 247 nm when the solvent was changed from ultrapure water to THF (Fig. S7b). When the solvent was changed from ultrapure water to DMSO, DMF or THF, the absorption peak at 390 nm were cleaved to 411 and 464 nm, 350 and 462 nm and 350 and 452 nm, respectively (Fig. 5d). The lifetimes of the Ni-CDs gradually decreased with decreasing solvent polarity (Fig. S7c), and their average lifetimes in ultrapure water, DMSO, DMF and THF were 9.15 ns, 7.98 ns, 6.95 ns and 6.34 ns, respectively (Table S5). The above results reveal that the Ni-CDs undergo deprotonation in different solvent. The TEM images of the Ni-CDs in DMSO, DMF and THF showed significant aggregation with average sizes of 3.9 nm (Fig. S7d), 5.1 nm (Fig. S7e) and 4.3 nm (Fig. S7f), respectively, all larger than their average size in ultrapure water of 2.1 nm (Fig. 1a). Based on the above results, it can be concluded that the sensing mechanism of the Ni-CDs to water in organic solvents is mainly due to the deprotonation of the Ni-CDs in organic solvents, which aggregated into larger sizes and presents two absorption peaks. The increased water content weakens the solvent effect and chemical bonding, leading to a better dispersion of the Ni-CDs and allowing their fluorescence to be recovered (Scheme 2).

To investigate the significance of Ni doping, we synthesized and explores the CDs without the Ni doping (m-CDs). First, the potential of m-CDs for pH and water sensing was investigated. The FL intensity of m-CDs varied with increasing pH ( Fig. 6a), and the fitted sigmoidal curve was fitted as y = 15.2478 + 513.8055/(1 + 10((pH −8.632)/0.4134)) with the R2 and pKa of 0.9968 and 8.63, respectively. The pH sensor based on m-CDs has a narrow detection range of 8.0–10.0. When the water content in the organic solvents DMSO (Fig. 6b), DMF (Fig. 6c) and THF (Fig. 6d) exceeded 30%, m-CDs appeared a fluorescence peak at 490 nm. The above results indicate that although m-CDs are responsive to pH and water, the sensitivity is extremely poor, limiting their application. We hypothesize that the different optical properties and structures of m-CDs and Ni-CDs lead to significant differences in their sensitivity to pH and water. The m-CDs have optimal excitation and emission wavelengths of 385 nm and 490 nm (Fig. 6e) that are essentially close to those of the Ni-CDs, respectively, indicating that Ni doping does not change the luminescence position of the CDs but has an important effect on QYs (vide infra). The m-CDs had four distinct UV–vis absorption peaks at 209 nm, 238 nm, 289 nm and 390 nm (blank line in Fig. 6e). After the doping of Ni in m-CDs, the absorption peak at 238 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectrum of m-CDs was red-shifted to 256 nm, suggesting a change in the aromatic sp2 bond of the Ni-CDs (Fig. S8). In addition, the absorbance of the Ni-CDs at 390 nm was significantly higher than that of m-CDs (Fig. S8), illustrating that Ni doping affected the surface groups of the Ni-CDs, resulting in a significantly higher QY of the Ni-CDs (30.3%, Fig. S2c) than that of m-CDs (21.4%, Fig. S9a), which is agreement with previous reports [16]. As displayed in Table S6, the content of H atoms in the Ni-CDs (57.1%) is higher than that of m-CDs (44.4%), which makes the Ni-CDs highly susceptible to deprotonation and more sensitive to pH and water. For the FTIR spectra of m-CDs (red line in Fig. 1c) and Ni-CDs (black line in Fig. 1c), the O-H/N-H (3601–3014 cm−1) stretching vibration of the Ni-CDs is stronger than that of m-CDs, demonstrating that there are more H atoms or O-H/N-H in the Ni-CDs. In conclusion, the doping of Ni changed the structure and surface state of the Ni-CDs, thereby increasing the H-atom content and QY of the Ni-CDs to enable more sensitive detection of pH and water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The bioimaging in live cells and zebrafish
ce:section.ce:para: To better explore the application of the Ni-CDs in bioimaging, we first investigated their toxicity to HeLa cells using the MTT method. When the concentration of the Ni-CDs was as high as 200 μg mL−1, the viability of HeLa cells remained above 90% ( Fig. 7a), indicating the low cytotoxicity and bioimaging potential of the Ni-CDs. As seen in Fig. 7b, HeLa cells incubated with the Ni-CDs at normal pH (7.4) can emit green fluorescence without any change in their morphology. In addition, the effect of the Ni-CDs at different pHs on the imaging ability of HeLa cells was also determined, and the results indicated that HeLa cells stained by the Ni-CDs at pH 6.0 displayed stronger green fluorescence, while the intensity of green fluorescence emitted by HeLa cells decreased when the pH was increased to 7.0, and when the pH was 9.0, HeLa cells almost did not emit green fluorescence (Fig. 7c and S10a). Zebrafish has good optical transparency and is considered to be the optimal choice as an animal model for bioimaging studies. Bright green fluorescence was visible in zebrafish treated with the Ni-CDs at pH 7.4 (Fig. 7d), indicating that the Ni-CDs are tissue-permeable and capable of imaging zebrafish in vivo. Similarly, zebrafish were incubated in the Ni-CDs with different pHs (6.0, 7.0 and 9.0) to evaluate the sensitivity of the Ni-CDs to pH in vivo (Fig. 7e and S10b). The green fluorescence emitted in the zebrafish body gradually decreased with the change of pH from 6.0 to 9.0. The fluorescence results of the Ni-CDs in HeLa cells and zebrafish at different pHs are consistent with those obtained by fluorometry (vide supra). Combined with the low cytotoxicity of the Ni-CDs, it fully indicates that the Ni-CDs have good imaging capabilities and can be used to monitor pH changes in cells and zabrafish.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The information encryption of the Ni-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Inspired by the Ni-CDs for pH and water sensing, we further constructed hydrogels based on the Ni-CDs and explored their applications in information encryption. HCl and alkaline vapor (NH3) were used as information encryption media. When Ni-CDs/hydrogels were exposed to NH3, the pH gradually increased as the exposure time was extended from 0 min to 5 min, resulting in a gradual decrease in the FL intensity of Ni-CDs/hydrogels under UV irradiation ( Fig. 8a). On the contrary, the FL intensity of Ni-CDs/hydrogels under UV irradiation was gradually enhanced after HCl treatment for 5 min (Fig. 8a). Although the color change of Ni-CDs/hydrogels after NH3 or HCl treatment was obvious under UV irradiation, their color hardly changed under visible light irradiation (Fig. 8b). Since organic solvents are not volatile, representative organic pure solvents including DMSO, DMF and THF were dropped directly on the surface of the Ni-CDs/hydrogel to simulate the change of water content by permeation time. With the extension of immersion time, the color of Ni-CDs/hydrogels changed from dark green to blue under UV light irradiation after 2 h of DMSO treatment (Fig. 8c). The changes of Ni-CDs/hydrogels after DMF treatment were similar to those of Ni-CDs/hydrogels after DMSO treatment. Since THF is volatile, its effect on the fluorescence color of Ni-CDs/hydrogels was not regular and the results were relatively unstable. The fluorescence color of Ni-CDs/hydrogels treated with THF for 2 h changed from light green to blue and then to bright green under UV irradiation (Fig. 8c), indicating that Ni-CDs/hydrogels can be reused after the THF evaporates. Similar to the NH3/HCl-treated Ni-CDs/hydrogels, the organic solvents-treated Ni-CDs/hydrogels showed almost no color change under visible light irradiation. Based on the above results, we believe that Ni-CDs/hydrogels demonstrate attractive application prospects in the field of information encryption as a low-cost and reusable hydrogel.

In summary, the green fluorescent (30.3%) Ni-CDs were prepared by a simple and efficient hydrothermal method. Based on the deprotonation and aggregation mechanism of the Ni-CDs, a Ni-CDs-based fluorescence and smartphone dual-mode sensor for pH and water detection was established. Low cytotoxicity and high pH sensitivity have allowed the Ni-CDs to be successfully used for cellular and zebrafish imaging. Considering that the Ni-CDs have sensing properties for pH and water, Ni-CDs/hydrogels were further constructed, which have important application potential in the field of information encryption. In conclusion, the Ni-CDs with excellent optical properties, high sensitivity and low toxicity are ideal candidates for building multi-target sensing platforms, bioimaging and information encryption.
